State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Legal Assistance                Administration          Executive/Legal          Enforcement          Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                    322-5660                  322-5901                322-6441                322-6444


December 14, 1982


Maurice F. O'Shea
Redevelopment Agency Counsel/
City Attorney
16420 Colorado Avenue
Paramount, CA 90723

                                                    Re:    Request for Advice
                                                           Our No. A-82-183

Dear Mr. O'Shea:

     Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of
Mr. Mosier, Mr. Boogaard and yourself.  The facts, as I
understand them, are that you are the counsel for the
Paramount Redevelopment Agency (hereinafter "Agency") and
Mr. Mosier and Mr. Boogaard are members of the Paramount
City Council and serve on the Board of the Agency.  The
Agency is in the process of preparing preliminary plans for
the downtown redevelopment project.  This project con-
templates the extensive acquisition and redevelopment of
property in the area.  Councilmember Mosier and you own
property in the redevelopment area that may be acquired by
the agency.  Councilmember Boogaard rents the property that
you own; for the last ten years, he has operated a
furniture store in the building on that property.

     In your letter, you posed several questions regarding
the ability of each of you to make or participate in the
making of redevelopment decisions.

     The Political Reform Act prevents a local government
official from making, participating in the making of in any
way attempting to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.  (Government Code Section
8700.1/)  An official has a financial interest within

________________
     1/  Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Maurice F. O'Spea
December 14, 1982
Page 2


the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,2/
on any real property in which the public official has a
direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000.
(Section 87103(b))

Since Mr. Mosier and you each own real property in the
area worth over $1,000, you both have interests in real
property which could potentially be affected by Agency
decisions. If the value of Mr. Boogaard's leasehold is
over $1,000, he also has an interest in real property in
the area which could be affected by Agency decisions. The
value of a leasehold is calculated by multiplying the
amount of the rent by the period during which Mr. Boogaard
can reasonably be expected to occupy the premises.3/

To determine whether or not a particular decision will
have a material financial effect on any of these real
property interests, you must determine whether the decision
will affect the real property interest in the manner
described in 2 California Administrative Code Section
87102(b)(2)(See enclosed copy.) This determination must
be made on a case-by-case basis.

You indicated in your letter that there is a
possibility that the pieces of property owned by Mr. Mosier
and you will be acquired by the Redevelopment Agency. Each
of you must abstain from any decisions concerning the
acquisition of your respective pieces of property; it is
reasonably foreseeable that the effect of a decision to

2/ For the meaning of "making, participating in the
making or in any way attempting to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision," see the
enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)-(e).
For an explanation of the "public generally" exception, see
the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.

3/ For example, if he pays $400 per month in rent
and he has a reasonable expectation of remaining in the
building for at least three months, then he has a real
property interest worth over $1,000.

Maurice F. O'Shea
December 14, 1982
Page 3


purchase your property will be material.4/ Similarly,
Mr. Boogaarda must disqualify himself from a decision if it
is foreseeable that his leasehold will be materially
affected.5/

If the Redevelopment Agency decides to purchase either
Mr. Mosier's piece of property or yours, the Agency will
become a source of income to the seller.6/ Although
Section 82030(b)(2) exempts salary and reimbursements
received from a governmental entity from the definition of
income, there is no exception for proceeds received as a
result of a sale to an agency. Thus, the Redevelopment
Agency would be a source of income of over $250 which must
be reported on the seller's Statement of Economic
Interests.

However, even if the Agency does purchase your
property, and thus becomes a source of income, you will not
have to disqualify yourself from decisions affecting the
Agency. An official must ordinarily disqualify himself
from any decisions which will affect any source of income
of $250 or more in the past 12 months, unless the decision
will affect the source in the same manner as it affects the
public generally. In this particular situation, where your
source of income is the Agency making the decisions, the
Agency is considered to represent the public generally.
Therefore, disqualification would not be required.7/

4/ An official who must disqualify himself can
appear before his Agency to represent his own interests in
the purchase.

5/ Assuming that Mr. Boogaard's leasehold is worth
more than $1,000.

6/ The term "income" includes the proceeds from any
sale. Section 82030.

7/ However, you may have to disqualify yourself on Agency
decisions that affect things other than the Agency itself.

My analysis, as to the application of the "public generally"
exception, applies only if you are making decisions as a member
of the Agency board. The result may be different if you are a
member of a different body that is making decisions affecting the
Redevelopment Agency.

Maurice F. O'Shea
December 14, 1982
Page 4


     In addition to the interests in real property discussed
above, Mr. Boogaard has an investment interest of over
$1,000 in his furniture store.  Thus, he may not make or
participate in the making of a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on this investment.  (Section 87103(a).)
He should consult the enclosed copy of 2 California
Administrative Code for a description of what constitutes a
"material effect" on a business entity.8/

     I hope I have answered all of your questions.  If I can be
of any further help to you, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km
Enclosures

_________________________

8/ Please also note that Mr. Boogaard is a source of
income to you of $250 or more because of the rent he pays you.
You must abstain from a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material effect on him.  (See
Sections 87100, 87103 and the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702(b)(3)(C).)

Redevelopment Agency
of the

CITY OF PARAMOUNT

16420 COLORADO AVENUE, PARAMOUNT, CALIFORNIA 90723

(213) 634-2123

October 29, 1982


Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO Box 807
Sacramento, CA  94804

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

I am City Attorney and Agency Counsel for the Paramount
Redevelopment Agency.

At this time, I am requesting an advice letter regarding
conflict of interests dealing with two (2) board members
of the Agency and myself.

The Paramount Redevelopment Agency is in the process of
preparing preliminary plans, generally referred to as the
"downtown project":

One Councilman - Board Member owns property in the
project area. (Councilman "A").

Councilman - Board Member "B", has a month-to-month
lease on the property owned by me.

I, Agency Counsel and City Attorney (Mr. "C") own
property in the project area.

Previous disclosures as to such interests were filed as
required by law.

I have, along with the Councilman "A", who owns property
in the project area, declared a conflict of interests on
all matters dealing with the "downtown project." The downtown
project contemplates extensive acquisition of properties
and redevelopment thereof, including possibly the subject
properties.

Councilman - Board Member "B" that is leasing month-to-month,
has been a tenant of the property over ten (10) years and
operates same as a retail furniture store; to date he has

not declared a conflict of interests on the "downtown project."

ISSUES:

1. Does the Councilman - Board Member "B", who is on a
month-to-month tenancy, on a lease from Agency Counsel
Mr. "C", who has declared a conflict, also have a conflict
of interests?

2. In the event the Redevelopment Agency determines and
does acquire Councilman "A"s" property and/or Agency Counsel
Mr. "C"s" property:

Does the declared conflict and the disqualification thereby
terminate, and thereafter enable Councilman "A" and Counsel
Mr. "C" to participate in decisions on subsequent matters
dealing with the "downtown project"?

Alternately: If the Agency determines not to acquire Council-
man "A"s" property, and/or Mr. "C"s" property, may they
thereafter participate in decisions on subsequent matters?

Your courteous attention to this matter is appreciated.
Should you need more information please contact me at your
convenience at (213) 630-5913.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MAURICE F. O'SHEA
Agency Counsel
City Attorney

MFO:da

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

November 3, 1982

Maurice P. O'Shea
City Attorney
City of Paramount
16420 Colorado Avenue
Paramount, CA 90723

Re: A-82-183

Dear Mr. O'Shea:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:pin
Enclosure

Redevelopment Agency
of the
CITY OF PARAMOUNT
16420 COLORADO AVENUE, PARAMOUNT, CALIFORNIA 90723
(213) 634-2123

October 29, 1982


Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO Box 807
Sacramento, CA 94804

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

I am City Attorney and Agency Counsel for the Paramount
Redevelopment Agency.

At this time, I am requesting an advice letter regarding
conflict of interests dealing with two (2) board members
of the Agency and myself.

The Paramount Redevelopment Agency is in the process of
preparing preliminary plans, generally referred to as the
"downtown project":

One Councilman - Board Member owns property in the
project area. (Councilman "A").

Councilman - Board Member "B", has a month-to-month
lease on the property owned by me.

I, Agency Counsel and City Attorney (Mr. "C") own
property in the project area.

Previous disclosures as to such interests were filed as
required by law.

I have, along with the Councilman "A", who owns property
in the project area, declared a conflict of interests on
all matters dealing with the "downtown project." The downtown
project contemplates extensive acquisition of properties
and redevelopment thereof, including possibly the subject
properties.

Councilman - Board Member "B" that is leasing month-to-month,
has been a tenant of the property over ten (10) years and
operates same as a retail furniture store; to date he has

not declared a conflict of interests on the "downtown project."

ISSUES:

1. Does the Councilman - Board Member "B", who is on a
month-to-month tenancy, on a lease from Agency Counsel
Mr. "C", who has declared a conflict, also have a conflict
of interests?

2. In the event the Redevelopment Agency determines and
does acquire Councilman "A's" property and/or Agency Council
Mr. "C's", property:

Does the declared conflict and the disqualification thereby
terminate, and thereafter enable Councilman "A" and Counsel
Mr. "C" to participate in decisions on subsequent matters
dealing with the "downtown project"?

Alternately: If the Agency determines not to acquire Council-
man "A's" property, and/nor Mr. "C's" property, may they
thereafter participate in decisions on subsequent matters?

Your courteous attention to this matter is appreciated.
Should you need more information please contact me at your
convenience at (213) 630-5913.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MAURICE F. O'SHEA
Agency Counsel
City Attorney

MFO:da

See also 18702(g)


June 5, 1991

A-21-06-080

Carl K. Newton
Burke, Williams & Sorensen
777 Wilshire Blvd., Suite 1300
Los Angeles, CA 90017

Re: Request for Written Advice re: Councilman Bob Fry

Dear Mr. Newton:

Thank you for your recent letter requesting advice relative to the duties of Councilman Bob Fry under the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000-91014 1/). As stated in your letter, the facts are as follows:

Conrock Company owns property at 791 North Todd Avenue in the City of Azusa. Said property is zoned M2, Consolidated Fencing, a lessee, from Conrock Company proposes to install a new use on the property consisting of concrete products manufacturing. The land use "concrete products manufacturing" is a permitted use within the M2 zone of the City subject to the requirement that a Conditional Use Permit first be obtained. A hearing was held before the Planning Commission of the City on the Conditional Use Permit Application which granted the same by adopting Resolution No. 1740. Before the action of the Planning Commission in granting the Conditional Use Permit became final, the matter was brought before the City Council which voted to appeal the approval of the conditional Use Permit. Councilman Bob Fry abstained from voting on the question of appealing the approval of the Conditional Use Permit based upon my advice that he had a potential conflict of interest by reason of the location of property and his business which he owns almost directly across the street from the property under application.

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Carl K. Newton
June 5, 1981
Page Two


Todd Avenue is a north/south street in the City of
Azusa. The property under application for the Conditional Use Permit is located on the west side of Todd
Avenue. Councilman Robert Fry owns property on which
is situated a cabinet shop business which he also owns
located on the east side of Todd Avenue. The south
property line of Councilman Fry's property is 230 feet
north of the north property line of the subject property,
if said line were extended across Todd Avenue to the
east side of the street. The business owned and operated
by Councilman Fry is a cabinet shop known as Pasquire
Cabinets. The business is a sole proprietorship. The
property on which Psquire Cabinets is situated is owned
in joint tenancy by Councilman Fry and his wife.

Both sides of Todd Avenue are zoned 42. Except
for some vacant land the majority of both sides of Todd
Avenue are improved with manufacturing uses except for
an old non-conforming trailer park on the east side of
Todd Avenue south of an extension of the southerly
property line of the subject property.

It also appears safe to conclude that the business
entity and the real property owned by Councilman Robert
Fry are each worth in excess of $1,000. In addition,
the business owned by Councilman Fry has produced income
to him amounting $250 or more in value during the
prior twelve month period. It further appears that the
business known as Enquire Cabinets is solely owned and
operated by Councilman Fry.

In addition, you have advised me by telephone that the
property in question is currently vacant, as is the property
directly to the south of it. Across the street from Mr. Fry's
property is a contractor's storage yard and further north is
a lumberyard. Directly adjacent to his property is light
manufacturing.

Government Code Section 87100 prohibits public officials
from participating in making governmental decisions in which
they have a financial interest. Section 87101 defines the
term "financial interest." As recognized in the last paragraph
of your statement of facts, quoted above, Councilman Fry has
a financial interest in his cabinet shop and property within
the meaning of Section 87101(a), (b), (c) and (d).

Carl K. Newton
June 5, 1981
Page Three


The issues are whether the effect of the decision in
question upon his financial interest will be material and
whether it will differ from the effect upon the public generally.

Material Financial Effect

The term "material financial effect" is further discussed
in our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.2  The
decision in question may increase or decrease the income
which Mr. Fry's property produces, the value of his property,
the income to his business, or the income to himself (since
he is a sole proprietor).  The dollar guidelines may then
be applied to each of these aspects of the facts.  The effect
of the decision will be material if it is expected to increase
or decrease: (1) his income by $100 or more or (2) his
property's value by more than $10,000 or by more than $1,000
(where the increase or decrease represents one-half of one
percent of the existing value of the property); or (3) his
business' gross revenues by $100,000 or more or by $1,000 or
more (where the increase or decrease is one-percent of the
current gross revenues); or (4) his business' net income by
$50,000 or by $1,000 (where the increase or decrease is one-
half of one percent).  These are factual questions which
really must be answered by Mr. Fry.  However, both the $100
income and the $1,000 property value test (assuming the
property value does not exceed $200,000) are fairly low
thresholds to meet.

For additional guidance, your attention is directed to
some of the Commission's opinions.  Most directly on point
is our Opinion No. 76-089, Gillmor, 3 FPPC 38 (April 6,
1977),2/ which involves a very similar circumstance.  In
Gillmor the Commission had a few more facts to aid it in
concluding that there was a material financial effect.
However, the analysis in Gillmor should benefit you in assisting Mr. Fry. (See p.4-5 of that opinion.  Specifically,
your attention is also directed to footnote 4 of the opinion.)
See also our Opinion No. 76-005, Owen, 2 FPPC 77 (June 2,
1976),2/ which discusses both materiality and the "public
generally" issue.

Public Generally

Upon examining the Owen opinion, the Gillmor opinion
and the Brown opinion, (4 FPPC 19, Opinion No. 77-024 [February 7, 1978]),2/ which also involves land and questions of

2/  Copy enclosed for your convenience.

Carl K. Newton
June 5, 1981
Page Four


the public generally, it seems clear that, in the instant
case, the effect upon Councilman Fry's financial interest is
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.27

Lastly, you ask advice regarding the parameters of
"materiality" and "public generally" in the geographical
sense. We cannot answer such hypothetical questions. As to
each such situation, the guidelines discussed above would
have to be applied to the specific facts. However, we believe
that the opinions mentioned herein provide substantial guidance
to the Council in this regard.

Should more detailed advice be desired, we would need
to know more details about the foreseeable effects on the
property owned by Mr. Fry and more about the foreseeable
effects upon his business. Should you, the Council, or
Mr. Fry wish further advice, please contact us. Mr. Fry
feels secure that his business will not be affected, nor
will his property values be affected; therefore, he can
participate so long as his analysis is correct.

Sincerely,


Robert F. Feldigh
Counsel
Legal Division

Enclosures

MARTIN J. BURKE
ROYAL M. SORENSEN
GEORGE W. TACKABURY
JANET BRADSHAW JR.
MARK C. ALLEN, JR.
RICHARD P. FERGUSON
MICHAEL C. GOULD
CARL K. NEWTON
J. ROBERT FLANDRICK
DENNIS P. BURKE
LELAND C. COLLEY
COLIN LENNARD
R. MICHAEL WILKINSON
BRIAN J. SEERY
THOMAS J. FEELEY
ROBERT W. LONG
BRIAN A. PIERIK
THOMAS H. DOWNEY
KATHERINE F. STONE
CHARLES M. CALDERON
BEATRICE JOY BRAUN
PETER M. THOMSON
TERESA N. TRACY
PHILLIP R. NEWELL
HAROLD A. BRIDGES

TELEPHONE
(213) 485-0101


OF COUNSEL
DWIGHT A. NEWELL
GEORGE W. WAKEFIELD

LAW OFFICES

BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN
SUITE 3300
307 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

May 29, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request on Behalf of City of Azusa for Opinion
    or Advice of Commission Under Section 83114,
    Government Code.

Dear Chairman and Members of the Commission:

    I represent the City of Azusa as City Attorney.
I am writing on behalf of the City with the request that
you provide either an opinion or advice under the provisions
of Section 83114, Government Code relative to a question
which has arisen concerning a possible conflict of interest.

    The opinion request is made relative to the
following facts:

    Conrock Company owns property at 791 North
Todd Avenue in the City of Azusa. Said property is zoned
M2. Consolidated Fencing, a Lessee, from Conrock Company
proposes to install a new use on the property consisting
of concrete products manufacturing. The land use "concrete
products manufacturing" is a permitted use within the M2 zone
of the City subject to the requirement that a Conditional
Use Permit first be obtained. A hearing was held before
the Planning Commission of the City on the Conditional Use
Permit Application which granted the same by adopting
Resolution No. 1740. Before the action of the Planning
Commission in granting the Conditional Use Permit became
final, the matter was reviewed by the City Council which
voted to appeal the approval of the Conditional Use Permit.

HARRY C. WILLIAMS
(312-1967)

Fair Political Practices Commission
Chairman and Members of the Commission
Page 2
May 29, 1981

Councilman Bob Fry abstained from voting on the question of
appealing the approval of the Conditional Use Permit based
upon my advice that he had a potential conflict of interest
by reason of the location of property and his business which
he owns almost directly across the street from the property
under application.

Todd Avenue is a north/south street in the City
of Azusa. The property under application for the Conditional
Use Permit is located on the west side of Todd Avenue.
Councilman Robert Fry owns property on which is situated a
cabinet shop business which he also owns located on the east
side of Todd Avenue. The south property line of Councilman
Fry's property is 230 feet north of the north property line
of the subject property, if said line were extended across
Todd Avenue to the east side of the street. The business
owned and operated by Councilman Fry is a cabinet shop known
as Esquire Cabinets. The business is a sole proprietorship.
The property on which Esquire Cabinets is situated is owned
in joint tenancy by Councilman Fry and his wife.

Both sides of Todd Avenue are zoned M2. Except for
some vacant land the majority of both sides of Todd Avenue
are improved with manufacturing uses except for an old non-
conforming trailer park on the east side of Todd Avenue south
of an extension of the southerly property line of the subject
property.

It also appears safe to conclude that the business
entity and the real property owned by Councilman Robert Fry
are each worth in excess of $1,000. In addition, the
business owned by Councilman Fry has produced income to him
aggregating $250 or more in value during the prior twelve
month period. It further appears that the business known
as Esquire Cabinets is solely owned and operated by Councilman Fry.

The City Council has directed that I request from
the Commission an opinion or advice relative to whether
Councilman Robert Fry may participate in the proceedings in-
volving the Conditional Use Permit application for the
Conrock Company property described above, or does he have a
financial interest by reason of his ownership of his business
and property in the proximity of the subject property which

Fair Political Practices Commission
Chairman and Members of the Commission
Page 2
May 29, 1981


prevents such participation. The City Council would also
appreciate guidance if you determine that a decision on
the Conditional Use Permit Application will have a material
financial effect distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally on Councilman Fry's business or property,
what distance or circumstance of removal of said business
or property from the subject property would place it in a
classification of not being materially affected financially
by the proposed project.

     I trust that the foregoing provides you with
sufficient facts upon which to base either an opinion or
advice to the City relative to the questions posed. If you
should need any further information regarding this matter,
please don't hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        CARL K. NEWTON
                                        OF BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN

cc:  Mayor and Members of the Council
     John Dangleis, City Administrator

sections.question:
1. Does the Councilman - Board Member "B", who is on a month-to-month tenancy, on a lease from Agency Counsel Mr. "C", who has declared a conflict, also have a conflict of interests?

2. In the event the Redevelopment Agency determines and does acquire Councilman "A's" property and/or Agency Council Mr. "C's", property: Does the declared conflict and the disqualification thereby terminate, and thereafter enable Councilman "A" and Counsel Mr. "C" to participate in decisions on subsequent matters dealing with the "downtown project"? Alternately: If the Agency determines not to acquire Councilman "A's" property, and/nor Mr. "C's" property, may they thereafter participate in decisions on subsequent matters?
sections.conclusion: Board Member B must disqualify himself from decisions affecting his leasehold interest if it exceeds $1,000 in value and the decision would have material effect on it, and must also disqualify from decisions affecting his business interest in the furniture store. After property acquisition, Agency Counsel and Board Member A become sources of income to themselves through the Agency sale proceeds, but need not disqualify under the "public generally" exception since the Agency becomes the source. However, they may need to disqualify on other Agency decisions not affecting the Agency itself. If properties are not acquired, the original conflicts remain and disqualification continues. Board Member B is also a source of income to O'Shea through rent payments, requiring O'Shea's disqualification from decisions materially affecting B.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Board Member B (a month-to-month tenant operating a furniture store) has a conflict of interest regarding redevelopment decisions, and whether the conflicts of Board Member A and Agency Counsel O'Shea terminate after their properties are acquired or not acquired by the Agency.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Board Member B must disqualify himself from decisions materially affecting his leasehold and business interests if their values exceed $1,000. After the Agency acquires properties from O'Shea and Board Member A, they may participate in decisions affecting the Agency itself (under the "public generally" exception) but must remain disqualified from other decisions affecting the Agency. If properties are not acquired, the original disqualification obligations continue.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request (October 29, 1982) followed by acknowledgment (November 3, 1982) and FPPC response advice letter (December 14, 1982). Question and conclusion sections extracted from the advice letter portion. The incoming request is duplicated in the document. High confidence in extraction due to clear structure and explicit question/conclusion sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does the Councilman - Board Member "B", who is on a month-to-month tenancy, on a lease from Agency Counsel Mr. "C", who has declared a conflict, also have a conflict of interests?

2. In the event the Redevelopment Agency determines and does acquire Councilman "A's" property and/or Agency Council Mr. "C's", property: Does the declared conflict and the disqualification thereby terminate, and thereafter enable Councilman "A" and Counsel Mr. "C" to participate in decisions on subsequent matters dealing with the "downtown project"? Alternately: If the Agency determines not to acquire Councilman "A's" property, and/nor Mr. "C's" property, may they thereafter participate in decisions on subsequent matters?

CONCLUSION: Board Member B must disqualify himself from decisions affecting his leasehold interest if it exceeds $1,000 in value and the decision would have material effect on it, and must also disqualify from decisions affecting his business interest in the furniture store. After property acquisition, Agency Counsel and Board Member A become sources of income to themselves through the Agency sale proceeds, but need not disqualify under the "public generally" exception since the Agency becomes the source. However, they may need to disqualify on other Agency decisions not affecting the Agency itself. If properties are not acquired, the original conflicts remain and disqualification continues. Board Member B is also a source of income to O'Shea through rent payments, requiring O'Shea's disqualification from decisions materially affecting B.

O'Shea Redevelopment Agency Counsel/ City Attorney 16420 Colorado Avenue Paramount, CA 90723 Re: Request for Advice Our No. A-82-183 Dear Mr. O'Shea: Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Mosier, Mr. Boogaard and yourself. The facts, as I understand them, are that you are the counsel for the Paramount Redevelopment Agency (hereinafter "Agency") and Mr. Mosier and Mr. Boogaard are members of the Paramount City Council and serve on the Board of the Agency. The Agency is in the process of preparing preliminary plans for the downtown redevelopment project. This project contemplates the extensive acquisition and redevelopment of property in the area. Councilmember Mosier and you own property in the redevelopment area that may be acquired by the agency. Councilmember Boogaard rents the property that you own; for the last ten years, he has operated a furniture store in the building on that property. In your letter, you posed several questions regarding the ability of each of you to make or participate in the making of redevelopment decisions. The Political Reform Act prevents a local government official from making, participating in the making or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 87100.1/) An official has a financial interest within 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,2/ on any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000. (Section 87103(b)) Since Mr. Mosier and you each own real property in the area worth over $1,000, you both have interests in real property which could potentially be affected by Agency decisions. If the value of Mr. Boogaard's leasehold is over $1,000, he also has an interest in real property in the area which could be affected by Agency decisions. The value of a leasehold is calculated by multiplying the amount of the rent by the period during which Mr. Boogaard can reasonably be expected to occupy the premises.3/ To determine whether or not a particular decision will have a material financial effect on any of these real property interests, you must determine whether the decision will affect the real property interest in the manner described in 2 California Administrative Code Section 87102(b)(2). (See enclosed copy.) This determination must be made on a case-by-case basis. You indicated in your letter that there is a possibility that the pieces of property owned by Mr. Mosier and you will be acquired by the Redevelopment Agency. Each of you must abstain from any decisions concerning the acquisition of your respective pieces of property; it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of a decision to 2/ For the meaning of "making, participating in the
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing conflict of interest issues for three Paramount Redevelopment Agency officials regarding property acquisitions in a downtown redevelopment project, clarifying disqualification requirements under Government Code Sections 87100-87103 for property and business interests, and the application of the "public generally" exception when Agency decisions affect the Agency as a source of income.
